title: Dog Walking Behavior Correction (2026) – Fix Lunging, Zigzagging & Refusing
seoTitle: Dog Walking Behavior Correction – Lunging, Zigzagging Fixes | GetPawsy
seoDescription: Fix common dog walking behavior problems: lunging at other dogs, zigzagging, stopping, and refusing to walk. Positive methods only.
h1Override: Dog Walking Behavior Correction – Fix Lunging, Zigzagging & Refusing
excerpt: Solutions for lunging at other dogs, zigzag walking, stopping mid-walk, and leash refusal. Positive reinforcement corrections for every problem.
quickAnswer: Most walking behavior problems stem from three causes: reactivity (lunging), over-stimulation (zigzagging), or fear (refusing). Each requires a different approach — lunging needs counter-conditioning at distance, zigzagging needs engagement training, and refusing needs confidence building.

faq.question: Why does my dog lunge at other dogs on leash?
faq.answer: Leash reactivity is usually frustration or fear, not aggression. The leash prevents your dog from doing what they want (greet or flee), causing an outburst. Counter-conditioning at safe distances teaches calm responses over 3–6 weeks.

sections.heading: Lunging & Reactivity
sections.content:
**The Problem:** Dog barks, lunges, or fixates on other dogs, people, or animals during walks.

**The Fix: Counter-Conditioning**
1. Identify your dog's 'threshold distance' — how far away a trigger can be without reaction
2. At that distance, when your dog notices the trigger → feed high-value treats continuously
3. When trigger passes → treats stop
4. Over weeks, gradually decrease distance

**Equipment:** Front-clip harness + 6-foot leash for control. Browse [leash control tools →](/collections/dog-leash-control).

For the foundation protocol, read our [Leash Training Guide →](/guides/leash-training-dog-step-by-step).

sections.heading: Zigzagging, Stopping & Refusing
sections.content:
**Zigzagging:** Your dog is sniff-exploring, not walking with purpose. Solution: Allow 'sniff breaks' as rewards for walking nicely. Walk 30 steps on loose leash → 'go sniff' for 30 seconds.

**Stopping Mid-Walk:** Usually sensory overload, discomfort, or fear. Check paws for injuries, check harness fit, and consider if the environment is too stimulating. Start with quieter routes.

**Refusing to Walk:** Common in puppies and newly adopted dogs. Never drag. Crouch, encourage, use treats to lure forward. Build positive associations gradually.

For all walking equipment needs, browse our [leash & control collection →](/collections/dog-leash-control) and [training accessories →](/collections/dog-training-accessories).
